rawText: On 8 July 2026 NutraIngredients reported an RCT published in Frontiers in Physiology in which 29 healthy men took 15 g/day of BodyBalance (Gelita AG) collagen peptide alongside 12 weeks of high-load resistance training, significantly increasing intramuscular collagen type I content versus placebo without an increase in fibroblast numbers, implying increased activity of existing fibroblasts. Exact effect sizes, confidence intervals and p-values are not stated in the accessible portion of the source. Collagen is marketed overwhelmingly to women aged 35–60 for skin, hair, nails and joints, whereas this trial enrolled 29 men only and its endpoint is a muscle-biopsy structural biomarker, not skin appearance, joint pain or strength; the 15 g/day dose is well above the 2.5–10 g/day typical of consumer collagen products. · ANGLES: (1) Who a study was done in is a buyer's first question — collagen sold to women, tested in 29 men, measured in muscle tissue. (2) 15 g/day versus the dose on the tub — a dose-gap check people can run themselves. (3) The training did the work — the finding is about the response to high-load resistance training, not a powder on its own. · APP CONNECTION: check dose and check evidence fit — paste a collagen product and the app compares its dose and claimed benefit against the population and endpoint actually studied. · SECONDARY: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2026.1839695/full (Frontiers in Physiology, doi 10.3389/fphys.2026.1839695, grade A — seen in search results but NOT fetched; verify before quoting any figure) · SOURCE GRADE: B (trade press reporting on a supplier's branded ingredient), resting on a grade A paper. · NOTE: the 2.5–10 g/day consumer-dose range is context, not a figure from this article — source it before publishing.
